codeSnapshot.label: cross_validation.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    KFold, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score,
    LeaveOneOut, GroupKFold, TimeSeriesSplit
)
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge

X, y = np.random.randn(500, 20), np.random.randn(500)

# --- Standard k-fold CV ---
kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
scores = cross_val_score(Ridge(alpha=1.0), X, y, cv=kf, scoring='neg_mean_squared_error')
print(f'5-fold CV MSE: {-scores.mean():.4f} ± {scores.std():.4f}')

# --- Stratified k-fold (preserves class balance, classification only) ---
X_clf, y_clf = np.random.randn(500, 20), np.random.randint(0, 3, 500)
skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
scores_clf = cross_val_score(LogisticRegression(), X_clf, y_clf, cv=skf)
print(f'Stratified 5-fold Accuracy: {scores_clf.mean():.4f}')

# --- Leave-One-Out CV (k = n, maximum computation) ---
loo = LeaveOneOut()
# Only use on small datasets (n < 200) — n evaluations needed
loo_scores = cross_val_score(Ridge(alpha=1.0), X[:50], y[:50], cv=loo,
                              scoring='neg_mean_squared_error')
print(f'LOO-CV MSE: {-loo_scores.mean():.4f}')

# --- Group k-fold (prevent data leakage from grouped data) ---
# Example: each patient has multiple samples — don't split patient across folds
groups = np.repeat(np.arange(50), 10)  # 50 patients, 10 samples each
gkf = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)
for fold, (train_idx, val_idx) in enumerate(gkf.split(X, y, groups=groups)):
    # No patient appears in both train and val within a fold
    assert len(set(groups[train_idx]) & set(groups[val_idx])) == 0

# --- Nested CV: outer for performance estimate, inner for HPO ---
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
inner_cv = KFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=1)
outer_cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
clf = GridSearchCV(Ridge(), param_grid={'alpha': [0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0]}, cv=inner_cv)
nested_scores = cross_val_score(clf, X, y, cv=outer_cv,
                                scoring='neg_mean_squared_error')
print(f'Nested CV MSE: {-nested_scores.mean():.4f} ± {nested_scores.std():.4f}')

eli3: Cross-validation is a smarter way to estimate how well your model will perform on new data. Instead of using just one fixed validation set, you divide the data into k groups (folds), train k times (each time using a different fold as validation), and average the results.

Why is this better? With a single validation set, you get a noisy estimate — you might have gotten lucky or unlucky with that particular split. By averaging k evaluations, you get a much more stable and reliable estimate of performance.

k=5 or k=10 is the standard choice — this is not arbitrary. Kohavi (1995) showed experimentally that k=10 gives the best bias-variance tradeoff for accuracy estimation. Leave-one-out (k=n) is unbiased but has very high variance and is expensive for large datasets.

whenWhy.title: Small datasets where a fixed val set is too noisy
whenWhy.body: For n<1000, a 15% validation set has only ~150 examples — too few for stable performance estimates. k-fold CV uses all n examples for training (across folds) and all n examples for evaluation (each used once as validation), giving lower-variance estimates. k=10-fold CV is the recommended default (Kohavi 1995): low enough variance, manageable computation (10× training runs). For very small datasets (n<100), leave-one-out CV is preferred — every example is used as a validation point exactly once.

whenWhy.title: Model selection and hyperparameter tuning
whenWhy.body: Cross-validation is the standard method for comparing models and selecting hyperparameters. sklearn's GridSearchCV and RandomizedSearchCV both use cross-validation internally. Nested CV: outer k-fold for unbiased performance estimate, inner k-fold for hyperparameter selection. Without nested CV, the selected hyperparameters are optimised to the outer validation folds, causing overoptimistic performance estimates. This bias grows with the number of hyperparameter configurations tried.

whenWhy.title: Grouped and structured data
whenWhy.body: Group k-fold prevents data leakage from correlated groups: if data contains multiple samples per patient, subject, or document, standard k-fold might put some samples from the same patient in both train and val, inflating performance. GroupKFold ensures all samples from a group are in the same fold. Similarly, StratifiedKFold preserves class balance in each fold for imbalanced classification — prevents a fold happening to contain mostly majority-class examples.

deepDiveIntro: k-fold cross-validation splits the dataset into k equal parts, trains the model on k-1 parts, evaluates on the remaining part, and repeats k times. The reported CV error is the average loss across all k held-out folds. Leave-one-out (k=n) uses every single example as its own test set exactly once. CV estimates the expected error on new data drawn from the same distribution as the training set. [1]

Choosing k involves a bias-variance tradeoff. Leave-one-out CV is nearly unbiased because each model trains on almost all the data, but it has high variance because the k models are trained on nearly identical datasets and give correlated estimates. k=5 or k=10 introduces a small bias (training on only 80-90% of data) but yields much more stable estimates. Kohavi (1995) showed empirically that k=10 hits the sweet spot across many datasets. [2]

deepDive.heading: k-Fold CV Procedure and Computation
deepDive.body: Shuffle the data, split into k equal folds, then for each fold train on the remaining k-1 folds and evaluate on the held-out fold. Report the average loss and its standard error across folds. The total computation is k times the cost of one training run. For linear models there is a shortcut: leave-one-out error can be computed from a single matrix inversion using the hat matrix, avoiding n separate training runs. [1]

deepDive.heading: Nested Cross-Validation
deepDive.body: Using the same CV folds to both select hyperparameters and report performance biases the result — the selected hyperparameters are implicitly tuned to those folds. Nested CV fixes this by separating concerns: an outer k-fold loop provides an unbiased performance estimate, while an inner j-fold loop handles hyperparameter selection within each outer training set. A typical setup uses 5 outer folds and 3 inner folds with 20 random hyperparameter configurations, costing 300 training runs total. [1]

deepDive.heading: Stratified and Group CV
deepDive.body: Stratified k-fold ensures each fold has the same class proportions as the full dataset — critical for imbalanced problems where a random split might put all rare-class examples in one fold. Group k-fold ensures no group (e.g. all samples from one patient or one document) appears in both training and validation within any fold, preventing data leakage from correlated observations. Always check whether your data has a group structure before using standard k-fold. [1]

youtubeLabel: Cross-Validation: k-Fold, Nested, and Best Practices
takeaways: CV(f̂) = (1/k)·ΣᵢL(f̂₋ᵢ,Dᵢ) — average loss over k held-out folds. [1]
k=5 or k=10 recommended: lower bias than k=n (LOOCV) with much lower variance. [2]
Nested CV: outer folds for performance estimate, inner folds for hyperparameter search — unbiased. [1]
StratifiedKFold for classification: preserves class distribution in each fold. [1]
GroupKFold when data has correlated groups (patients, documents): prevents leakage. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does cross-validation underestimate error for time series data?
edgeCases.a: Standard k-fold CV shuffles data randomly, so future data leaks into training folds. For a time series at time t, the validation fold may contain timestamps before some training fold timestamps — the model is trained on data from after the validation period. This mimics lookahead, producing optimistically biased CV estimates. Solution: sklearn TimeSeriesSplit — training folds are always chronologically before the validation fold, with an optional gap parameter to prevent direct lookback. Alternatively, blocked time series CV: create k contiguous blocks chronologically, always using earlier blocks for training and later blocks for validation.

edgeCases.q: What is the '.632 bootstrap' and when is it better than k-fold?
edgeCases.a: Bootstrap validation: sample n points with replacement from D to form D_boot (training), evaluate on examples not in D_boot (approximately 36.8% of D). The naive bootstrap error is pessimistic (only ~63.2% of data used for training). The .632 estimator corrects this: Err_.632 = 0.368·err_train + 0.632·err_boot. The '.632+' estimator (Efron & Tibshirani, 1997) further adjusts for overfit models. Bootstrap is preferred over LOOCV when: (1) the metric is difficult to compute analytically; (2) the dataset has complex structure (bootstrap provides more stable fold compositions). In practice, k=10 fold CV is more widely used due to lower computational cost and simpler implementation.

sources.label: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman (2009) — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Chapter 7 (Springer)
Kohavi R. (1995) — A study of cross-validation and bootstrap for accuracy estimation and model selection, IJCAI
